replies.text:
@Nighted-mb2my The statement that "Cleopatra's half-sister was black, the first pharaoh in the first pyramid was black, and all tested mummies were also black" is not supported by reliable scientific evidence. 

Here’s a nuanced consideration: 

What do the facts say? 
Cleopatra VII was part of the Ptolemaic dynasty, which originated in Greece. 
Her family spoke Greek and maintained Greek culture. 
There is no reliable evidence that Cleopatra or her half-sister were black. 
The first pharaoh in the first pyramid was likely Djoser, who had the step pyramid built in Saqqara. He lived in the 27th century BC. 
The population of Egypt at that time was a mix of North African and sub-Saharan influences, but there is no evidence that Djoser was "black" in today’s ethnic sense.

Pharaoh mummies: 
Genetic analyses, such as the study from 2017 on mummies from Abusir el-Meleq, show that ancient Egyptian mummies are genetically closer to present-day people from the Middle East and Europe than to Sub-Saharan Africans. 
This contradicts the blanket statement that all Pharaohs were 'black'.

Your statement seems to be more ideological or provocative rather than based on scientific grounds. 😞

​​​@maple0369I enjoyed this quite a bit and didn't enjoy Thunderbolts much.

F4 didn't have agonizing Marvel Humor constantly undercutting all the drama. There were some plot issues and inconsistencies, that's for sure, but common enough to the genre. Silver Surfer was cool, it actually works to have her female since it changes her relationship with one member of the family; didn't affect anything negatively.

It was absolutely beautiful to look at. I'm sure there was a bit of CGI jank, but the retrofuturistic design of the world was transcendent. I'm sad we won't get to see (much) more of this world.

I thought Galactus was great. He had a sense of underestimating the Fantastic Four and Sue, forcing him back made sense. Marvel comic readers have always spouted about how powerful she really is and we saw it here. Her powers you could say were amplified through sheer emotion to protect her child. Halfway through the movie when she gives that speech to the world outside the baxter building I can't remember if she said her mum or dad, but they told Sue that they would move heaven and earth for their children to protect them.
